author: @Mechveteran011
text: They destroy everything they touch. When I was a kid, old timers would sometimes say things about our beloved 13%ers, and I thought they were just racist. Then I did a stint in the military, and I was exposed to people from all over this great nation. Thats when I learned why those old men of my youth would say the things they did. Story time: I remember one time we had a rare Friday evening formation because somebody did something they weren't suposed to do and the whole platoon had to pay for it. Fair enough, it happens. Well, one of *them* (he was a new troop that had arrived maybe a month or two prior) kept bugging me for a ride back to the barracks from where we had to go for the formation. But because of the way he usually acted, I didnt want anything to do with him and didnt want him in my car. But he kept bugging me and begging "c'mon man, why cant you just give me a ride?".. And I thought, give the guy a chance, dont be reeecist. "Alright alright fine, get in." We get back to the barracks and I tell him to make sure and lock the passenger door (and watched him do it before I got out of the car. He gets out moments after me and closes the door to my car as he leaves. I head to my barracks room to get changed back into civi's. I head back out to my car maybe 20 minutes later and go to pick a CD out of my giant CD book (some of yall know what I'm talking about lol) that I had spent the last 3 years of my life filling with my favorite albums. Couldn't find it anywhere. Then it hit me and I looked at the passenger door lock... unlocked. I went straight to the kids room, I was an E4 and he was an E1. I could see that his light was on when I got there via the peephole in the door, and after I loudly knocked, it turned off and the random noises I had previously heard coming from his room suddenly stopped. I told him I knew he was in there, demanded he open the door and told him I wasnt leaving until he did, which he eventually did. I walked in and started opening his wall lockers and looking all over his side of the barracks room (there was 2 troops to each room). Dude didnt say a word, not "what the hell are you doing/who do you think you are.. nothing. Almost like he already knew damn well why I was there. I then saw one of my CDs on his bed and asked him about it "oh, thats mine". "You listen to the Goo Goo Dolls, huh?" I found my giant case of CDs shoved under his bed behind a duffle bag. I'm guessing he turned the light off when he saw me through the peep hole, and then went to hide the thing and forgot he had left one of the CDs on the bed. Anyway, that was one of many experiences with them that I had in the military that opened my eyes. Dont get me wrong, it certainly wasnt all of them by any means, one of my favorite SSGTs was one of them. SSGT Williams, I still miss that guy and I hope he's doing well in life. He took me under his wing when I too was but a lowly E1 and was having a hard time adjusting to military life in a foreign country. It wasn't all of them, but boy oh boy was it a lot of them. Point is, stereotypes are *most definitely* earned, not given. And as I've grown older and experienced more and more life, I've learned to always watch my back with a certain type, and that has paid off in dividends more than a few times.
